svg.text: Description
THE WRETCHED OF THE EARTH · DATA PROVENANCE
Entity
link
description
The Wretched of the Earth
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wretched_of_the_Earth
A landmark work of postcolonial theory written by Frantz
Fanon during the Algerian War of Independence. The book
analyses the psychological and political effects of
colonialism and argues for the necessity of
decolonisation.
Frantz Fanon
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frantz_Fanon
Martinican psychiatrist, philosopher and revolutionary.
Born in Fort-de-France, Martinique, Fanon worked as a
psychiatrist in Algeria, where he joined the FLN (Front
de Libération Nationale). His work combines
psychoanalysis, Marxism and anticolonial thought. He died
of leukemia at 36, shortly after completing The Wretched
of the Earth.
Jean-Paul Sartre
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre
French existentialist philosopher and writer. Sartre
wrote the controversial preface to the 1961 edition of
The Wretched of the Earth, in which he endorsed Fanon's
argument on the liberatory potential of anticolonial
violence. His involvement brought the book to wider
European attention and sparked significant debate.
Homi K. Bhabha
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homi_K._Bhabha
Indian-British postcolonial theorist and professor at
Harvard University. Bhabha wrote the foreword to the 2004
edition of The Wretched of the Earth, offering a
reinterpretation of Fanon's legacy through the lens of
poststructuralism. His key concepts include hybridity,
mimicry and ambivalence.
Aimé Césaire
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aimé_Césaire
Martinican poet, playwright and politician. One of the
founders of the Négritude movement, Césaire was a major
intellectual influence on Fanon, who studied under him.
His Discourse on Colonialism (1950) shares many of
Fanon's concerns about the dehumanising effects of
colonial rule.
Grove Press
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grove_Press
Grove Press published the first English translation of
The Wretched of the Earth in 1963, translated by
Constance Farrington. Known for publishing politically
radical and avant-garde literature.
Algeria
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algeria
A country in North Africa, formerly a French colony from
Martinique
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martinique
A Caribbean island and French overseas territory in the
Lesser Antilles.
Algerian Revolution
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algerian_War
The war of independence fought between Algeria and
France, also known as the Algerian War. It was the direct
historical context in which Fanon wrote The Wretched of
the Earth. The conflict resulted in Algerian independence
in 1962 after eight years of armed struggle and
significant loss of life on both sides.
Second Congress of Black Writers and
Artists
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Congress_of_Black_Writers_a
nd_Artists
An international gathering of Black intellectuals and
artists held in Rome in 1959, organised by the journal
Présence Africaine. Fanon delivered a speech at the
congress which later became the basis for key sections of
The Wretched of the Earth, particularly his analysis of
national culture and the role of intellectuals in
decolonisation.
Colonialism
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colonialism
A system of political and economic domination in which
one country exerts control over another, exploiting its
resources and people. In The Wretched of the Earth, Fanon
analyses colonialism not only as a political structure
but as a totalising system that destroys the identity and
psychology of the colonised, dividing the world into the
coloniser's zone and the colonised zone.
Decolonisation
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decolonization
The process by which colonised peoples reclaim political
independence and cultural identity from colonial powers.
For Fanon, decolonisation is not merely a political event
but a complete transformation of the social order — a
process that must involve the active participation of the
colonised masses, particularly the peasantry and the
lumpenproletariat.
Négritude
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Négritude
A literary and intellectual movement founded in the 1930s
by French-speaking African and Caribbean writers,
including Aimé Césaire and Léopold Sédar Senghor.
Négritude celebrated Black African culture and identity
as a response to French colonial assimilation. Fanon
critiqued the movement in The Wretched of the Earth,
arguing that it risked essentialising Blackness and was
ultimately insufficient as a political programme for
decolonisation.
"On Violence"
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wretched_of_the_Earth
The opening chapter of The Wretched of the Earth and its
most controversial section. Fanon argues that colonial
rule is itself founded on violence, and that anticolonial
violence can serve as a psychological and political act
of liberation for the colonised. The chapter drew sharp
reactions — both endorsement from Sartre and fierce
criticism from liberal thinkers across Europe and North
America.
